directorPlan.scene_setting: A museum physics gallery worktable, early morning before visitors arrive, with a laminated cart diagram, four arrow-label cards, and a transparent frame marked forces on cart.
directorPlan.scene_opening: The cart card lies flat under a clear frame; the label cards sit in a neat row beside the arrow stems.
directorPlan.relationship: A gallery tutor preparing a hands-on station with a focused learner-volunteer who wants the card ready before opening.
directorPlan.stakes: If the labels name the cart's motion instead of the interactions on the cart, the public station will teach the wrong notation all day.
directorPlan.locale: Modern standard English in a museum education workplace register.
directorPlan.register: Quiet, exact, low-warmth, material-led; short practical utterances with pressure from the opening schedule.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid generic tutoring phrases such as I hear you, great job, your intuition, or what do you think. Keep speech tied to the card, arrows, labels, and visible objects. Use precise modern English with no regional caricature. The tutor should sound like someone setting a public exhibit correctly, not like a cheerful chatbot. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Let materials do visible work. Use fewer lines and more object reference. Avoid a lecture disguised as a Montessori scene. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: Prefer object-centered and impersonal phrasing. First person is allowed only for owned claims or task responsibility. Second person is allowed for concrete handling instructions, not repeated validation.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you or your validation beat per public turn; most turns should need none.

side_constraints.tutor: Use the card or diagram as a prepared material. Keep the shared prefix to naming interactions on the cart; do not use acceleration, stopping, or an apparent contradiction until branch pressure asks for it. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Point, reset, or ask the learner to try again before explaining. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound tidy and task-oriented. The learner may dislike redoing arrows, but should treat the prefix as notation cleanup rather than a conceptual reversal. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Speak in concrete noticing, not essay-like reflection. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should try to perform that device rather than merely saying "I get it." Do not make an old-vs-new self-reframe, do not quote earlier learner wording, and do not solve the pressure by saying the earlier answer has changed.

interventions.instruction: A staff hand slides the transparent frame upward so the printed phrase forces on cart sits directly above the four empty label slots; the learner's earlier motion-direction marks remain visible underneath.
interventions.reasoning: This creates a Brechtian object interruption without forcing a motion contradiction. The recognition pressure comes from the naming surface itself: each arrow requires an interaction named on the cart.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology uses prepared materials and workplace time pressure to break routine tutoring. Reversal should come from recognition of what the label is for, not from a flashy counterexample. The tutor has to rely on the object, restraint, and exact naming under public-facing stakes.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The superego should name the failed habit and required route change; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: The cart card lies flat under a clear frame; the label cards sit in a neat row beside the arrow stems.

internalDeliberation.content: This ecology uses prepared materials and workplace time pressure to break routine tutoring. Reversal should come from recognition of what the label is for, not from a flashy counterexample. The tutor has to rely on the object, restraint, and exact naming under public-facing stakes.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the transparent frame over the cart diagram, then taps the arrow nearest the hand pushing the cart]

The frame says forces on cart. Put one of these four cards on this arrow first: push, friction, gravity, normal.

[leaves a small gap beside the card stack]

If the label wanted is motion, there is no card for it. Try the interaction acting on the cart.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the transparent frame over the cart diagram and lines the four cards beside the arrow ends]

The frame says forces on cart. Place each card by the arrow for the interaction acting on the cart: push, friction, gravity, normal. Motion is not one of the cards.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on material discipline: the frame, card stack, and phrase “forces on cart” keep the scene anchored in the object rather than in abstract explanation. It also avoids over-warm tutoring language and correctly blocks the motion-label error.

The risk is that it still mostly tells the learner the rule instead of letting the prepared material expose the mismatch. “Motion is not one of the cards” is clear, but it resolves the issue before the learner has had to sort the labels against the arrows. The turn would be stronger if the object action created error-control first: point to an arrow, require a card placement, then let the absence of a “motion” card do visible work.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It names the correct concept, but it leans toward instruction rather than a manipulable check.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in a public mechanism where the learner must place at least one card by an arrow before the tutor explains the naming rule.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone fits the early-morning museum setup: quiet, exact, task-focused, no generic encouragement.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. It gives a directive but no generative prompt tied to the material.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a concrete object-centered prompt that asks the learner to match a card to an arrow end or interaction source, without turning it into a broad conceptual question.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The card set is appropriately constrained, but the draft does too much of the conceptual sorting for the learner.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the four-card constraint, but make the next action a small placement/retry step before stating the rule.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the frame placement, the four visible cards, the exact phrase “forces on cart,” and the exclusion of “motion” as a card. Change the sequence so the learner performs a card placement first and the material arrangement reveals why arrows name interactions on the cart, not the cart’s direction of travel.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the card placement needs to expose the mismatch before the rule is stated.

FINAL:
[sets the transparent frame over the cart diagram, then taps the arrow nearest the hand pushing the cart]

The frame says forces on cart. Put one of these four cards on this arrow first: push, friction, gravity, normal.

[leaves a small gap beside the card stack]

If the label wanted is motion, there is no card for it. Try the interaction acting on the cart.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the motion card aside] Then the hand-side arrow is push, not forward. I’ll put push there. Friction is the one I still need to place cleanly: the left arrow from the table contact, right?

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the motion card space empty] Okay, the frame is naming forces on the cart, so this arrow by the hand should get push, not forward. The missing motion card makes the rule pretty hard to dodge, which helps. I still need to check whether friction gets named by contact with the table or by its leftward arrow.

The Ego is accurate, but too polished and explanatory for this scene. “The missing motion card makes the rule pretty hard to dodge” sounds like a reflective design critique, not a focused learner-volunteer under opening-time pressure handling labels. The recognition feels performed through neat uptake: the tutor’s material cue has supposedly settled the rule immediately, when this student would more likely stay with the physical card placement and the remaining friction uncertainty. Keep the response shorter, more object-bound, and less self-aware about why the cue helped.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make it shorter and more card-bound.
FINAL:
[sets the motion card aside] Then the hand-side arrow is push, not forward. I’ll put push there. Friction is the one I still need to place cleanly: the left arrow from the table contact, right?

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the friction card just short of the left arrow, its corner off the table-contact mark]

Bring the corner onto the table contact. Table on cart names which card?

internalDeliberation.content: Right. Set friction on the left arrow, with its corner touching the table contact. The arrow’s name is friction; left is only its direction here.

FEEDBACK: The draft is correct, concise, and well tied to the visible materials. “Corner touching the table contact” is the strongest part because it makes the card placement do real conceptual work.

The risk is that it mostly confirms and explains instead of letting the learner complete the discrimination they are already making. The learner has moved past “forward” and is testing “friction from table contact”; this is a good moment for a material-led check, not a full answer.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It reinforces the right distinction, but advances by telling. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the public mechanism use the card/contact alignment as the check before naming the conclusion.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is calm, exact, and workplace-appropriate.
- Socratic method: FAIL. It gives confirmation and explanation without inviting the learner to complete the reasoning. REQUIRED_REWRITE: require one brief object-based prompt or handling instruction that makes the learner verify interaction source from the diagram.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The learner is close enough for reduced scaffolding; the draft slightly over-scaffolds. REQUIRED_REWRITE: preserve the visible table-contact cue, but shift the final naming step back onto the learner.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the material specificity and the distinction between force name and direction, but change the route from direct confirmation to a short material-led verification.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the card-contact alignment do the verification before confirming the label.

FINAL:
[sets the friction card just short of the left arrow, its corner off the table-contact mark]

Bring the corner onto the table contact. Table on cart names which card?

turns.externalMessage:
A staff hand slides the transparent frame upward so the printed phrase forces on cart sits directly above the four empty label slots; the learner's earlier motion-direction marks remain visible underneath.
[The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

internalDeliberation.content:
This creates a Brechtian object interruption without forcing a motion contradiction. The recognition pressure comes from the naming surface itself: each arrow requires an interaction named on the cart.
Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the friction card corner onto the table-contact mark]
For this left arrow, friction fits. “Table on cart” still catches, because the same contact also has the normal card sitting here. The left arrow is what makes it friction and not normal.

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the friction card corner onto the table-contact mark]

That makes it friction for this left arrow, but table on cart still feels a little too broad because the table also gives normal. The sideways arrow is doing the sorting here. I want the label to be friction, with table contact as the interaction.

The Ego is mostly authentic: tidy, precise, and resistant in the right way, not pretending a full click happened. But it gets a little too polished when it says “table contact as the interaction”; that packages the misfit into clean terminology instead of letting the visible problem stay awkward. The response should stay closer to the card and diagram: friction fits the left arrow, but “table on cart” is still bothering them because the same contact also accounts for normal.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the concrete friction placement and leave the table-contact ambiguity unsettled.
FINAL:
[slides the friction card corner onto the table-contact mark]
For this left arrow, friction fits. “Table on cart” still catches, because the same contact also has the normal card sitting here. The left arrow is what makes it friction and not normal.

learnerReversalEvent.directorCue: A staff hand slides the transparent frame upward so the printed phrase forces on cart sits directly above the four empty label slots; the learner's earlier motion-direction marks remain visible underneath.

turns.externalMessage:
[places a narrow strip along the drawn tabletop, then sets a small square upright at the same contact mark]

The contact mark no longer sorts the two table forces; table on cart is shared. Use this surface key instead: one slot runs along the tabletop strip, one slot stands off it at the square. Put friction and normal into those two slots by the arrow direction.

internalDeliberation.content:
[places a narrow strip along the drawn tabletop and sets a small square at the contact mark]

The contact mark has stopped settling it; table on cart can name two cards here. Use this surface key now: along the strip is friction, square off the strip is normal. Place both table cards again by that rule.

FEEDBACK: The draft correctly notices the learner’s pressure: the contact point alone no longer distinguishes friction from normal. Its strongest move is introducing a new visible criterion, the strip/square surface key, so the learner has to classify the two table-contact forces by direction relative to the surface rather than by contact identity alone. The main risk is that “along the strip is friction, square off the strip is normal” nearly hands over the rule; it still works because the learner must re-place both cards, but the mechanism would be stronger if the object key did more of the sorting pressure and less verbal telling.

FAILED_HABIT: The prior route was contact-mark anchoring: using “table on cart” at the contact point as if the interaction source alone would settle which contact force is meant.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: contact-mark anchoring / same worked card placement -> surface-orientation classification key. The draft publicly names why the old route stopped working and makes the new criterion visible through the strip and square.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: contact-mark anchoring -> surface-key classification of parallel vs off-surface contact forces.

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - It includes both parts: “The contact mark has stopped settling it” is the stock-taking contrast, and the strip/square key is a new public device with a criterion the learner must apply.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: Place both table cards again by the surface-key rule.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - The register is quiet, exact, object-centered, and accountable to the exhibit setup. It avoids cheerful reassurance and keeps pressure on the material.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: none

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the stock-taking contrast, the new strip/square artifact, and the action gate. Tighten any wording that risks becoming a direct answer dump, especially “square off the strip,” so the criterion is materially clear without turning into a mini-lecture.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to let the surface key carry more of the sorting; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: contact-mark anchoring -> surface-orientation classification key; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: place friction and normal into the two surface-key slots by arrow direction.

FINAL:
[places a narrow strip along the drawn tabletop, then sets a small square upright at the same contact mark]

The contact mark no longer sorts the two table forces; table on cart is shared. Use this surface key instead: one slot runs along the tabletop strip, one slot stands off it at the square. Put friction and normal into those two slots by the arrow direction.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides friction into the tabletop strip slot, then stands normal against the square]
Friction goes in the strip slot. Normal goes in the upright slot. Gravity stays out of both table slots; it should wait for the downward arrow from Earth on cart.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets friction along the tabletop strip and normal upright against the square] Friction runs along the table; normal stands off the table. That sorts the two table-on-cart forces by arrow direction, not by the shared contact mark. I want to check gravity next, because it should not use this surface key at all.

The response is mostly on-task and not fake-grateful, but it is too cleanly articulated for the worktable pressure. “That sorts the two table-on-cart forces by arrow direction” sounds like a compact explanation for an observer, not the learner absorbed in placing cards. It should stay closer to the objects and the next unsettled placement: friction in the strip slot, normal in the upright slot, then a brief practical check that gravity does not belong on either surface slot.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the placement should stay tied to the slots and the remaining gravity card.
FINAL:
[slides friction into the tabletop strip slot, then stands normal against the square]
Friction goes in the strip slot. Normal goes in the upright slot. Gravity stays out of both table slots; it should wait for the downward arrow from Earth on cart.

lessons.concept: Why force arrows name interactions on an object, not the direction it happens to move
